content.problemStatement:
Chef recently organised a **Grand Feast** where he served nnn **different dishes** to his friends.

Each friend picked their favourite dish, and now Chef has a list of all the dishes chosen.

Chef is curious:


Which dishes were so popular that they were chosen by more than ⌊n/3⌋⌊n/3⌋⌊n/3⌋ friends? Print the dishes in increasing order of popularity of dish among the friends.
Can you help Chef find these **superstar dishes**?

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindSuperstarDishesfindSuperstarDishesfindSuperstarDishes – This function finds all dishes that appear more than ⌊n/3⌋ times.

Parameters
aaa : An array representing the dishes chosen by friends.
nnn : The number of dishes.
Return Value
This function **returns the array in ascending order**.
Constraints
1≤n≤5×1041 \leq n \leq 5 \times 10^41≤n≤5×104
−109≤a[i]≤109−10^9 \leq a[i] \leq 10^9−109≤a[i]≤109
The output may contain **at most two dishes** (by pigeonhole principle)

Input Format

The first line of each test case contains a single integer nnn — the number of dishes.
The next line contains nnn space-separated integers a[i]a[i]a[i] — the dishes chosen by friends.


Output Format

For each test case, print on a new line all dishes that were chosen by **more than ⌊n/3⌋ friends**, in **increasing order**.
If no such dish exists, print nothing for that test case.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the number of dishes. The second line contains N space‑separated integers a[i] — the dishes chosen by the friends.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print on a separate line all dish values that appear more than ⌊N/3⌋ times, in increasing order, separated by a single space. If no such dish exists, print an empty line.

solution.approach: The problem asks for all elements that occur more than ⌊N/3⌋ times. By the pigeonhole principle there can be at most two such elements. We can find them in O(N) time and O(1) extra space using a generalized Boyer‑Moore majority vote algorithm that keeps two candidate values and their counters. In the first pass we select possible candidates; in the second pass we count their real frequencies and keep those whose count exceeds N/3. Finally we sort the at‑most‑two answers and output them.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) for the whole input.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input array.
